section.title: Testing and Temptation
section.sectionRationale: James opens not with doctrine to argue but with life to live. He greets the scattered tribes, then plunges straight into the trials that press on them, teaching them to count trials joy because God uses them to mature faith. He distinguishes the trial that comes from outside, which God allows to test and strengthen, from the temptation that rises from within, which God never sends, and he anchors it all in the unchanging generosity of the Father of lights.
section.thesis: Trials from outside are given to mature a persevering faith, while temptation is born of our own desire and never comes from God; the Father of lights gives only good gifts, generous wisdom to those who ask, and new birth by the word of truth.
section.frameNote: Four movements: greeting to the scattered tribes; joy and wisdom in trials; the low and the high brought level; and the true source of temptation and of every good gift.

units.label: Greeting to the scattered tribes
units.note: a servant writes to the Dispersion

verses.text: James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ, To the twelve tribes of the Dispersion: Greetings.

units.label: Count it joy, ask for wisdom
units.note: trials test faith and grow perseverance

verses.text: Consider it pure joy, my brothers, when you encounter {{peirasmos|trials}} of many kinds,
because you know that the {{dokimion|testing}} of your faith develops perseverance.
Allow perseverance to finish its work, so that you may be mature and complete, not lacking anything.
Now if any of you lacks {{sophia|wisdom}}, he should ask God, who gives generously to all without finding fault, and it will be given to him.
But he must ask in faith, without doubting, because he who doubts is like a wave of the sea, blown and tossed by the wind.
That man should not expect to receive anything from the Lord.
He is a {{dipsychos|double-minded}} man, unstable in all his ways.

units.label: The low lifted, the high leveled

verses.text: The brother in humble circumstances should exult in his high position.
But the one who is rich should exult in his low position, because he will pass away like a flower of the field.
For the sun rises with scorching heat and withers the plant; its flower falls and its beauty is lost. So too, the rich man will fade away in the midst of his pursuits.
Blessed is the man who perseveres under trial, because when he has stood the test, he will receive the crown of life that God has promised to those who love Him.

units.label: Temptation within, good gifts from above
units.note: the Father of lights gives new birth

verses.text: When {{peirazo|tempted}}, no one should say, “God is tempting me.” For God cannot be tempted by evil, nor does He tempt anyone.
But each one is tempted when by his own {{epithymia|evil desires}} he is lured away and enticed.
Then after desire has conceived, it gives birth to sin; and sin, when it is full-grown, gives birth to death.
Do not be deceived, my beloved brothers.
Every good and perfect gift is from above, coming down from the {{paterphoton|Father of the heavenly lights}}, with whom there is no change or shifting shadow.
He chose to give us birth through the word of truth, that we would be a kind of firstfruits of His creation.

peirasmos.gloss: A trial or a test, and also a temptation; the one word covers both. Here it names the hardships that come upon believers from outside, which God allows to prove and strengthen faith. The same root shifts in verse 13 to inward temptation, and James is careful to keep the two apart.

dokimion.gloss: The testing or proving of something to show it is genuine, as fire proves gold. The testing of faith is not meant to destroy it but to demonstrate and refine it. What comes through the fire is a tried, dependable faith that produces perseverance.

sophia.gloss: Wisdom, the skill of living rightly before God, not mere cleverness. In trials the believer most needs wisdom, and James says God gives it generously to all who ask in faith. This wisdom from above is unfolded further in chapter three, and Scripture names Christ Himself as the wisdom of God.

dipsychos.gloss: Literally two-souled, double-minded; a word James seems to have coined. The double-minded person is divided, wanting God and the world at once, and so is unstable in everything. It is the opposite of the whole, single-hearted faith that asks God without wavering.

peirazo.gloss: To test or to tempt, the verb behind peirasmos. In verse 13 James uses it of inward solicitation to evil, which he insists never comes from God. God tests to strengthen, but He never entices anyone toward sin.

epithymia.gloss: Strong desire or craving, here evil desire. James traces sin to its true spring, not God and not mere circumstance, but our own disordered wanting. Desire conceives, gives birth to sin, and sin grown up brings death.

paterphoton.gloss: The Father of lights, the Maker of sun, moon, and stars. Unlike the shifting lights He made, He never changes and casts no turning shadow. From this unchanging, generous Father comes every good and perfect gift, above all the new birth by the word of truth.

basic.context: James is the Lord's brother, writing to Jewish Christians scattered far from home and under pressure. He does not open with a lecture; he opens with their trials. Count them joy, he says, because God is using them to grow a mature, persevering faith. If you lack wisdom to see it that way, ask God, who loves to give. Then he draws a sharp line: trials that come upon you from outside are one thing, but temptation to sin never comes from God. That rises from your own desire. God only ever gives good gifts, and the best gift is new life through His word.
basic.hermeneutics: James writes wisdom, like Proverbs, but in the form of a letter, so his commands are meant to be lived, not just admired. The key move to notice is one Greek word carrying two senses: trial and temptation. In verses 2 to 12 it means hardship that God allows to test faith; in verses 13 to 15 it means the pull toward sin that comes from within us. Reading them as the same thing leads to the error James is guarding against: blaming God for our sin.
basic.originalLanguages: The word peirasmos means both trial and temptation, which is why James works so hard to separate them. The testing, dokimion, is like the assaying of gold, meant to prove faith genuine. The unstable man is dipsychos, two-souled, wanting God and the world at once. Sin is traced to epithymia, our own craving, never to the Father of lights, who alone gives every good gift.
basic.typology: The crown of life promised to those who endure looks ahead to the reward Christ gives His faithful. The firstfruits language pictures believers as the first sheaf of a great harvest, a foretaste of the new creation. The word of truth that gives new birth is the gospel of Christ, the living and abiding word.
basic.christ: The wisdom James tells us to ask for is finally a person: Paul says Christ became for us wisdom from God. Jesus Himself faced real testing and never sinned, showing that trials need not produce sin. And it is through the word of truth, the gospel of Christ, that God gives us new birth as the firstfruits of His new creation.
basic.god: God is generous, giving wisdom to all who ask without scolding them for asking. He is unchanging, the Father of lights with no shadow of turning, so His goodness can be trusted in the dark. And He is holy: He cannot be tempted by evil and never tempts anyone, so we can never lay our sin at His door.
basic.crossReferences: Jesus taught the same joy in trial in the Beatitudes, Matthew 5:10-12. Paul traces the same chain from suffering to endurance to character in Romans 5:3-5. Peter speaks of faith tested by fire like gold in 1 Peter 1:6-7. And the Lord's Prayer asks not to be led into temptation, Matthew 6:13.
basic.oneStory: From the start the Bible shows God bringing His people through testing to maturity, as He did Israel in the wilderness. James sets the scattered church in that story: their trials are not meaningless but are shaping a persevering faith, while their sin is their own. The generous, unchanging Father who gives wisdom and new birth by His word is the same God who is remaking the world in Christ, and His people are its firstfruits.

context.literary: The letter opens with a brief greeting, then a tightly packed overture that sounds nearly every theme James will develop: trials, wisdom, wealth and poverty, prayer, and the danger of a divided heart. Verses 2 to 18 fall into two halves joined by a hinge word: the outward trial that matures faith, verses 2 to 12, and the inward temptation that must never be blamed on God, verses 13 to 18.
context.historical: James, widely held to be the brother of Jesus and leader of the Jerusalem church, writes to Jewish believers of the Dispersion, scattered by persecution and hardship. Many were poor and oppressed, some were pressed by wealthy landowners, and all faced the temptation to drift or to blame God. The letter reads like the practical wisdom of a pastor for a struggling, scattered flock.
context.canonical: James stands in the stream of biblical wisdom literature, echoing Proverbs and the teaching of Jesus, especially the Sermon on the Mount. Its theme of testing that matures faith runs from Israel in the wilderness through the Psalms to Peter and Paul, and its portrait of God as the unchanging, generous Father grounds the whole New Testament confidence in prayer.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To steady a scattered, suffering church by reframing trials as God's tool to mature faith, to urge them to ask God for wisdom in single-hearted faith, and to locate the source of temptation in human desire rather than in God, whose gifts are only good.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: James both describes and prescribes. He describes how trials work, how temptation is conceived, and how the Father gives; he prescribes counting trials joy, asking for wisdom without doubting, and refusing to blame God for sin. The commands are meant to shape daily discipleship, not merely to inform.

debates.question: How can we count trials pure joy?

views.label: Joy in the outcome, not the pain
views.case: James does not say the trial itself feels good, but that we can rejoice because of what God is doing through it, producing tested faith and perseverance. The joy is grounded in confident knowledge, because you know, not in pretending the hardship is pleasant.

views.label: A commanded, faith-shaped emotion
views.case: Others stress that this is a genuine command to reorient the heart, a joy that grows as faith learns to see trials from God's angle. It is not denial but a deliberate, Spirit-worked gladness that God is at work even here.

debates.bearing: Both agree the joy is real and rooted in what God accomplishes, not in the suffering itself. James is not asking believers to enjoy pain but to trust the God who redeems it.

debates.question: Does God tempt people, given that the same word means test?

views.label: No; God tests but never entices to evil
views.case: James is emphatic in verse 13: God cannot be tempted by evil and tempts no one. God may allow and use trials to prove faith, but the inward pull toward sin arises from our own desire, never from God.

views.label: The one word must be read by context
views.case: Because peirasmos covers both trial and temptation, the same event can be a test God allows and, if we yield, an occasion of temptation we own. The direction of blame is the point: strengthening is God's work, sinning is ours.

debates.bearing: Either way the pastoral point is fixed: we may thank God for maturing us through trials, but we can never excuse our sin by blaming Him. Sin is traced to our own craving.

study.originalLanguages: The whole passage turns on one flexible word, peirasmos, and its verb peirazo, which mean both trial and temptation. James exploits the overlap and then pulls the senses apart: the outward test, dokimion like the assaying of gold, is God's good work to prove faith and grow perseverance; the inward temptation is never God's doing but is born of epithymia, our own desire, which conceives sin and finally death. The wisdom we lack is sophia, the God-given skill of living, granted freely to the one who asks in undivided faith rather than as a dipsychos, a two-souled, wave-tossed doubter. Over it all stands the pater ton photon, the Father of lights, unchanging and generous, from whom comes every good gift and the new birth by the word of truth.

typology.type: The crown of life for those who endure
typology.fulfillment: The reward Christ promises to the faithful, the life He secured and gives, echoed in Revelation 2:10.
Believers as the first sheaf of the new creation harvest, guaranteed by Christ the firstfruits of the resurrection, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23.

typology.type: New birth by the word of truth
typology.fulfillment: The regeneration accomplished through the gospel of Christ, the living and abiding word, 1 Peter 1:23-25.

study.christ: James rarely names Jesus, yet Christ stands behind every line. The wisdom we are told to ask for is embodied in Him, for Christ became to us wisdom from God, 1 Corinthians 1:30. He endured every trial and temptation without sin, Hebrews 4:15, proving that testing need not breed sin and opening the way for us to stand. The crown of life He promises He first won, and the new birth by the word of truth is birth into His risen life, making us the firstfruits of the world He is remaking.
study.god: God is generous, giving wisdom to all who ask without reproach; He does not grudge or scold the needy who come. He is unchanging, the Father of lights with no variation or shadow of turning, so His goodness is utterly dependable in the darkest trial. And He is holy: He cannot be tempted by evil, and He tempts no one, so the blame for sin can never be shifted onto Him. Every good and perfect gift, up to and including new life, flows down from this steady, giving Father.

crossReferences.note: Jesus tells the persecuted to rejoice and be glad, the same counterintuitive joy in trial.
Paul's chain, suffering produces perseverance, character, and hope, parallels James on testing and maturity.
Faith tested by fire, more precious than gold, echoes the assaying pictured in dokimion.
The Lord gives wisdom; from His mouth come knowledge and understanding, behind James's promise in verse 5.
Christ became for us wisdom from God, the ultimate answer to the plea for wisdom.

study.oneStory: God has always led His people through the wilderness of testing to bring them to maturity, humbling and proving Israel to do them good in the end. James places the scattered church inside that ongoing story: their trials are not random cruelty but the refining hand of a generous, unchanging Father, while their temptations are their own to own and resist. The wisdom they need is Christ, the gifts they receive are all good, and the new birth by the word of truth marks them as the firstfruits of the new creation God is bringing in through His Son.

quiz.q: Why does James tell believers to count their trials pure joy?

options.text: Because the testing of faith produces perseverance and matures them.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4: the joy is in what God accomplishes through the trial.

options.text: Because endurance under trial leads to a crown of life.
options.note: True and promised in verse 12, though the immediate reason given is the maturing of faith.

options.text: Because the pain of trials is actually enjoyable.
options.note: James never says the suffering feels good; the joy is in its God-given fruit.

options.text: Because trials prove that God has abandoned them.
options.note: The opposite: trials are God's tool to strengthen, not a sign of abandonment.

quiz.q: What does James say about the person who asks God for wisdom?

options.text: He must ask in faith without doubting, or he is double-minded and unstable.
options.note: Verses 6 to 8: single-hearted faith, not a wave-tossed, divided heart.

options.text: God gives wisdom generously to all who ask, without finding fault.
options.note: True and the ground of the promise, though the point pressed is asking in faith.

options.text: He should first prove himself worthy before asking.
options.note: God gives without finding fault; worthiness is not the condition, faith is.

options.text: He should not bother God with his requests.
options.note: James commands the opposite: ask God, who loves to give.

quiz.q: Where does James say temptation to sin comes from?

options.text: From a person's own evil desire, which conceives sin and brings death.
options.note: Verses 14 to 15: the source is our own craving, never God.

options.text: From being lured away and enticed by what we crave.
options.note: True to verse 14, describing how the desire works.

options.text: From circumstances that leave us no real choice.
options.note: James locates the root in desire, not in circumstance removing responsibility.

options.text: From God, who tempts people to test them.
options.note: Verse 13 flatly denies this: God tempts no one.

quiz.q: How does James describe God in verse 17?

options.text: The Father of lights who gives every good gift and never changes.
options.note: Verse 17: unchanging, generous source of all good.

options.text: The generous giver from whom good gifts come down.
options.note: True, though it leaves out the emphasis on His unchanging steadiness.

options.text: A distant maker who set the world going and stepped back.
options.note: James stresses His ongoing, active generosity, not distance.

options.text: One whose favor shifts like the moving shadows.
options.note: The text says the exact opposite: no change or shifting shadow.

quiz.q: What is the double-minded person like, according to James?

options.text: A wave of the sea, blown and tossed, unstable in all his ways.
options.note: Verses 6 to 8: divided between God and the world, and so unsteady.

options.text: Someone divided who wants God and the world at the same time.
options.note: A fair description of dipsychos, two-souled instability.

options.text: Someone who simply has honest questions for God.
options.note: Honest questions are not the issue; a divided, doubting heart is.

options.text: Someone with strong, settled faith.
options.note: That is the opposite of the double-minded, wave-tossed person.

questions: James says to count trials joy because of what God does through them. Which trial in your life right now might God be using to grow a more mature, persevering faith?
When you lack wisdom, do you actually ask God for it, expecting Him to give generously, or do you try to figure it out on your own first?
Where are you tempted to be double-minded, wanting God and the world at once? What would single-hearted faith look like in that area?
James refuses to let us blame God for our sin, tracing it to our own desire. Is there a temptation you have been excusing rather than owning?
God is the unchanging Father of lights whose gifts are always good. How would resting in His steady goodness change the way you face this week?

translationNotes.note: Trials of many kinds renders peirasmos, a word that means both outward trial and inward temptation; James uses the same root in verse 13 in the sense of temptation, deliberately holding the two together and then pulling them apart.
Double-minded renders dipsychos, literally two-souled, a term found first in James; it pictures a person split between trusting God and clinging to the world.

Father of the heavenly lights refers to God as Maker of the celestial lights; a footnote offers no change or shadow of turning, the phrase contrasting God's constancy with the shifting shadows those lights cast.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for James 1:1-18. The greeting in verse 1, printed on separate lines in the BSB, is set as running prose here. No em or en dashes occur in this movement.
